text:
We describe an algorithm for finding galaxy clusters in large surveys of photometric data. We call the algorithm redMaPPer. The red-sequence is used as the defining characteristic of galaxies in clusters. The approach uses a self-training method to construct a good model of the red-sequence that requires only a small amount of training data (i.e. high quality spectroscopic redshifts). The approach also allows for full treatment of irregular survey masks and different survey depths. In addition to identifying clusters, the algorithm provides a large set of cluster centrals and halos of random points that can be used to do large scale structure studies. Finally, the method provides full probability distributions of redshifts, $P(z)$, for each cluster identification, and full probability distributions of centrals for each cluster halo that contains multiple galaxies with spectroscopic redshifts. The method is fast in computation and can be run on a CPU to find clusters for a full DES data set in about 500 hours of computing time.

We apply the algorithm to 10,400 deg 2 of SDSS DR8 data, resulting in a cluster catalog of 25,000 objects within the redshift range z ∈ [0.08, 0.55]. The accuracy of photometric redshifts is examined in great detail. We find the scatter in the photometric redshifts to be σ z ≈ 0.006 at z ≈ 0.1 increasing to σ z ≈ 0.02 at the survey limit of z ≈ 0.5 with the median value of |Δz|/(1+z) being 0.006. We investigate the connection between the richness of our clusters and some well known external mass indicators. We also investigate the contamination, completeness and projection contamination of our sample, which are all found to be less than 5% ensuring a very high purity of the sample. For further comparisons with X-ray and SZ detections, the reader is referred to the companion paper.
